purpose: Machine-readable implementation state for the AI_book-derived Theseus roadmap. This is the governed bridge between roadmap.md prose and registry-owned implementation work.

latest_claude_mining_reconciliation.coverage_result: The packet confirms existing planned_codex_test_backlog coverage for policy optimization, fast generation, GVR, semantic IR, verification bandwidth, VCM transaction certificates, Circle/Coil cyclic baselines, belief revision, and procedural memory.
latest_claude_mining_reconciliation.important_correction: DPO over accepted/rejected verifier pairs is no longer simply missing: a governed shadow update already moved private loss/preference-gap metrics and then failed strict private replay behaviorally. A later zero-credit semantic-operation value-construction canary preserved clean candidate integrity and emitted nontrivial transformer/hybrid rows, but still scored 0/2 private passes and exposed repeated max(max(... starvation plus shallow accumulator/type-handling failures. Future DPO/IPO/ORPO/KTO/SimPO or GRPO/RLVR work depends on direct prompt/signature body-token generation producing behavior-positive, non-fallback, non-template learned candidates beyond narrow simple-return replay.
latest_claude_mining_reconciliation.no_claim_boundary: Claude/AI_book technique recommendations are planning coverage, not evidence. Routers, tools, templates, semantic renderers, fallback bodies, public benchmark artifacts, and external runtime inference remain noncredit for learned generation.

weekly_focus_decision.recommendation: Spend the remaining weekly token budget primarily on Project Theseus while keeping AI_book as the specification, evidence ledger, and publication surface.

implementation_rules: Improve an existing registered surface before creating a new surface.
Bind every phase to an abstraction, implementation, and VIEA execution-spine hook before claiming it is wired.
Do not call a phase implemented from prose, private 1.0 scores, or self-declared flags alone.
Every completed phase must leave runnable gates, evidence reports, docs, and typed failure behavior.
Book-derived phases 13-19 are preserved; they may be sequenced, narrowed, or scaffolded, but not deleted from the roadmap.
At most one flagship implementation lane may be active at a time; other lanes can do maintenance, evidence sync, or narrow dependency work only.
Every capability statement must carry a support state: argument, prototype-backed, synthetic-test-backed, empirical-test-backed, or replayable-reference-backed.
Every authored AI_book chapter must map to a Theseus implementation slice with a minimum slice, beyond-SOTA endpoint, support-state target, no-claim boundary, and gate or fixture.

AI_book chapter-level Planned Codex tests are treated as missing technique obligations until a registered Theseus implementation has matched controls, negative controls, no-cheat audit, retained residuals, and explicit non-claims.
Negative, inconclusive, and regression evidence must remain attached to the claim or phase it constrains; do not replace a negative report with a nearby positive family.

Before training becomes the primary roadmap focus again, the book-reference core must be represented as runnable claim-ledger, replacement-transaction, authority/SCIF, assistant-product, and evidence-hygiene slices.

Claude/AI_book technique recommendations are coverage obligations, but if a technique has already produced clean negative behavior evidence, the roadmap must route to the lower-level wall before retrying it.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book Chapter 38 planned tests plus Claude fourth-pass mining
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: strict prompt/signature body-token replay must emit non-fallback, nontrivial, loadable candidates before another preference sweep can mean anything behaviorally
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: A bounded DPO/IPO/ORPO/KTO/SimPO comparison uses the same private accepted/rejected split, frozen reference checkpoint, heldout private verifier, reward-hacking probes, drift bounds, and rollback plan.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Loss or policy-gap movement is not capability. No public benchmark rows, answer templates, fallback bodies, routers, deterministic tools, or semantic renderers count as learned generation.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book Chapter 38 planned tests and LongRLVR source notes
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires usable learned candidates and a verifier-capacity ledger before online/group-relative reward optimization
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Run reward-present versus reward-removed ablations on private exact-verifier tasks with rollout budget, reward-hacking probes, drift bound, rollback receipt, verifier-cost ledger, and residual mining.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: A verifier-reward run cannot expand authority, cannot train on public benchmark artifacts, and cannot become a default route until heldout private behavior improves under replay.

planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires behavior-change leases with admissible feedback and per-target heldouts
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Planner, router, VCM selector, verifier policy, executor policy, and generation-mode selector updates each carry target policy, feedback source, authority effect, rollback, monitor window, and task-specific heldout checks.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: A policy update is not allowed to hide displaced verification, repair, human-cleanup cost, or authority expansion.

planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires candidate integrity and semantic atom mapping for actual generated failures
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Candidate states transition through generated, verified_exact, verified_lossy, repaired_exact, literal_fallback_noncredit, and quarantined with S/R/Q/G/V/E consistency checks, verifier receipts, repair cost, fallback count, and residual obligations.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Fallback or repaired output may be useful product behavior but never learned-generation promotion evidence unless independent integrity proves the learned candidate itself produced it.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book fast-generation chapters and multi-token prediction source notes
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires a nonzero behavior baseline so useful verified output per second can be measured
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: MTP is compared against a matched single-token AR control on the same private data, active compute, verifier, and decode budget, reporting accepted spans, verifier pass, useful-solution/sec, residuals, and regressions.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Proposed-token throughput, lower LM loss, or larger candidate count alone is not speed or capability evidence.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book fast-generation chapters plus Medusa/EAGLE/speculative/LayerSkip notes
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires generation-mode registry accounting and accepted-output scoring
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Each mode has a generation_mode_record with active compute, cache policy, verification cost, accepted-output quality, useful-solution/sec, fallback count, and matched AR baseline.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Fast-generation helpers do not count as learned correctness unless verifier-accepted behavior improves under matched controls.

planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires semantic IR atoms to be consumed by a localized repair or trainable AST/state-machine body-construction path that improves private replay, so scaffold/infill repair has behavior-positive obligations instead of whole-body rerolling
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: A sketch-first/infill candidate family is compared with AR one-shot decode on private tasks using the same verifier, candidate budget, and integrity audit, with localized repair residuals retained.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Sketch-first repair cannot claim learned generation if a deterministic renderer, hidden target field, public artifact, or fallback body supplies the answer.

planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires deployed resolver/compiler conformance beyond fixture-level VCM records
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Every materialized context packet carries source refs, omissions, loss contract, permitted uses, authority ceiling, taint/deletion closure, copy-on-write branch lineage, typed mandatory-miss faults, and consumer policy.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: A summary, cache hit, or retrieved packet cannot raise the authority ceiling of its source or claim model-native KV/prefix runtime parity without separate native lifecycle proof.

planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires route-level verifier capacity and residual-obligation ledgers
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Routing decisions include adequacy state, verification tax, displaced repair/human-cleanup cost, residual obligations, verifier budget consumption, and stronger-verifier escalation for high-risk work.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: More context, more candidates, or cheaper routing is not proof; high-risk claims pay verification cost or remain downgraded.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book Chapter 19 planned tests and truth-maintenance notes
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires immutable revision history attached to evidence transitions
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Claims support downgrade, split, merge, contradiction-link, retire, and replacement transitions without overwriting earlier evidence; contradictions remain linked to affected routes and docs.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: A newer green report cannot erase or relabel a negative result; changed belief must be represented as a revision transition.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book Chapter 24 and lookahead decoding planned tests
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires repeated real or replayable verified trajectories with preconditions and monitoring
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Verifier-passing repeated trajectories become procedural tool candidates with preconditions, postconditions, verifier receipt, monitoring, retirement, drift checks, and optional lookahead/trie decode source.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: A procedural tool or trie continuation can improve product behavior, but it is not learned body-token generation credit.

planned_codex_test_backlog.source_basis: AI_book Chapters 33-35 planned tests and Circle/Coil source notes
planned_codex_test_backlog.dependency: requires matched-compute substrate-adoption records and proof-boundary claims
planned_codex_test_backlog.acceptance_gate: Cyclic memory, KV-cache ring buffers, recurrence schedules, sparse-attention coverage, circulant/block-cyclic mixers, and MultiCoil/RoPE phase features are tested against dense/LoRA/RoPE/Mamba controls with matched data, matched active compute, negative controls, residuals, and falsification criteria.
planned_codex_test_backlog.no_claim_boundary: Circle proof-carrying fixtures prove only their formal boundary; they do not imply model quality, transfer, speed, memory, or ASI capability.

completion_status_semantics.implemented: Runnable, registry-bound, SCF-bound, documented, and emitting evidence through the canonical path.
completion_status_semantics.wired: Integrated into a canonical runtime path with at least one smoke trace, but not yet proven across every target profile.
completion_status_semantics.partial: Some supporting machinery exists, but missing records, hooks, gates, or product traces remain.
completion_status_semantics.missing: Concept exists in roadmap/book material but has no sufficient registered implementation.
completion_status_semantics.frozen: Useful concept or proof lane intentionally parked by current priorities or an explicit external-environment blocker such as unreachable trusted peers; it remains tracked and cannot be deleted silently.
completion_status_semantics.mapped: Chapter or concept is mapped to a registered implementation slice, but the slice is not yet enough for a capability or beyond-SOTA claim.
completion_status_semantics.support_state_required: Any claim beyond roadmap intent must name its support state and point at replayable evidence, negative controls, and non-claims.

lookahead/trie retrieval from verified procedural memory plus Medusa, EAGLE, speculative/self-speculative, early-exit/LayerSkip, Mamba-state, PagedAttention/KV-cache policy, and sketch-first/diffusion/LLaDA arbitrary-order repair experiments as governed generation modes with useful-solution-per-second accounting

substrate-adoption record for every capability technique: baseline, negative control, matched data/compute, falsification condition, residuals, reward-hacking probes, and non-claims
current_non_claims: Phase 10 is wired as architecture readiness for the practical transformer/hybrid survival lane, not as a model-quality or public-transfer win.

Current direct learned full-body behavior remains weak: fanout behavior is 5/22, C1 selected/pass-if-any is 0.0 on the clean transformer/hybrid private replay, and production route eligibility remains blocked.

The old Rust/template-like fanout inventory, action routers, semantic renderers, private ngrams, deterministic tools, and fallback returns remain diagnostic/tool baselines only and do not support learned-generation promotion claims.

The DPO shadow checkpoint replay is negative behavior evidence: it emitted zero learned candidate rows and 0/16 private heldout passes, so it remains quarantined and cannot support default routing, public transfer, or learned-generation promotion.

The direct body-emission and state-transition profiles are training evidence only: direct body emission matched 128/128 private rows and 8054 token positions, local-return closure matched 79/128 rows and 1967 positions, and closed-state transition matched 127/128 rows and 9637 positions, but the paired broad strict replays still have 0 main candidate rows or 0 behavior passes.

The guarded/default static relaxation is narrow behavior evidence on existing private simple-return replay only: it is not a public-transfer claim, not a promotion claim, and not evidence that loop/action or broad expression semantics are solved.

The strict decode hygiene and generated-state return/dependency guards block malformed isinstance first-argument chains, bare builtin type runtime values, constant-only conditions, top-level returns that ignore visible inputs, and missing finalizer tokens for model-created visible-input accumulators; this is pathology-removal evidence only, because the paired broad canary still emits 0 learned rows and 0/4 behavior passes.

The source-plan compatibility reranker is diagnostic adequacy evidence only: it uses prompt-visible source tags and moves first-plan choices on the strict two-row canary, but still emits zero manifest candidate rows and zero behavior passes. It is not learned-generation credit, not a renderer, and not a promotion route.

The body-action head is trainable structural-role evidence only: it activates over private body-continuation positions and can bias decode, but the first canary worsens heldout action loss/accuracy and strict replay still emits zero manifest rows with zero behavior passes. The two generated runtime-loaded verifier attempts are diagnostic shape movement, not admitted learned-generation improvement.

The body-operand/value binding head is trainable role evidence only: it improves heldout operand accuracy but not operand loss. When the existing learned-prefix adequacy route is enabled, the same checkpoint emits integrity-verified nontrivial transformer/hybrid rows, but behavior remains 0-pass and production routing fails closed on quality.

The operation-adequacy decode pass is diagnostic/ranking evidence only: it now reports missing_expected_update_call when the learned prefix predicts a call update but the body emits only assignment transforms, ranks prompt-visible operation evidence ahead of generic loop-score proxies, and lets prompt-visible operation tags propose operation-bearing branch/update tokens before the generic loop path preempts them. The bounded private canaries remain clean but behavior-zero: 4 integrity-verified generated rows, 0 integrity mismatches, nontrivial-return rate 1.0, and 0/2 private passes. book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.chapter_title: Capability Replacement and Rollback
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.part_title: Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_minimal_implementation: A replacement transaction schema with precheck, gate, commit, monitor, and rollback phases.
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_beyond_state_of_art: Capability replacement only works as a transaction control plane for the whole stack. Every candidate implementation enters as a transaction, not a hope: prior artifact, candidate artifact, field identity, authority ceiling, regression floor, evaluator boundary, canary scope, monitor window, rollback receipt, and residual inheritance are all first-class objects. In that end state, a capability can improve continuously without making the system forget what it used to know. Regressions follow the field forward, not the old implementation backward. Failed canaries become residuals. Rollbacks are rehearsed and owned. State migration is classified as reversible, compensatable, forked, or residual rather than treated as an implementation detail. A default route is therefore never just the latest winner; it is the candidate that survived the transaction while preserving the floor.
book_interfaces: SCF defines the field.
Benchmarks test behavior.
Evidence and changelog record the transition.
book_invariants: No replacement without prior and posterior artifacts.
Regressions stay attached.
Rollback metadata is required before promotion.

book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.chapter_title: Recursive Self-Improvement Boundaries
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.part_title: Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_minimal_implementation: A self-improvement protocol that can reject, quarantine, roll back, or retire a proposed change.
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_beyond_state_of_art: Recursive improvement belongs inside a governed improvement market, not a runaway loop. Components may propose patches, tools, routes, policy updates, benchmarks, teacher edits, and architecture changes, but promotion flows through stable fields, replacement transactions, independent evaluators, protected-invariant checks, boundary-delta review, monitor windows, and rollback ownership. In that end state, the system can improve faster because the rules are sharper. Cheap interventions are tried before expensive or irreversible ones. Verification, security, human review, and rollback budgets are protected from being "optimized away." Readiness gates expire when the frontier, architecture, source corpus, lifecycle state, or threat model changes. A candidate may generate evidence, but it cannot be the only judge of whether the evidence is enough.

Every accepted change remains auditable.

book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.chapter_title: Planning as a Control Layer: DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.part_title: Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_minimal_implementation: A public plan graph plus PlanForge DAG record surface with fixtures and validation for lifecycle states, dependency order, dispatch receipts, authority, capability tiers, adequacy contracts, cost-quality ledgers, residuals, invalid cycles, contract mismatch, requirement loss, unreceipted dispatch, approval bypass, scheduler-state boundaries, and runtime-replan delta shape, while leaving decomposition quality, selected-tier adequacy, route quality, live feedback handling, runtime replanning, and deployed scheduler behavior unclaimed.
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a governed scheduler and capacity allocator for cognitive work. It receives accepted command contracts, builds and maintains plan graphs, and decides which nodes are proposed, blocked, reviewable, dispatchable, dispatched, replanned, residualized, or stopped. It never owns memory, authority, verification, or side effects; it coordinates the handoffs that let those layers do their own jobs. Each node carries dependency order, context demand, authority state, capability tier, adequacy contract, verifier, cost envelope, route assignment, merge condition, escalation path, and residual behavior. Intelligence arbitrage is measured only after verification, repair, merge, human review, and residual cost are counted, so cheaper work is accepted only when it satisfies the node adequacy contract and remains useful to downstream dependents.

VCM supplies context packets and context adequacy records.
Routing consumes capability requirements, adequacy contracts, route assignments, and escalation paths.

book_invariants: Plans expose constraints and stop conditions.

Merge gates compare assumptions, source versions, authority ceilings, and quality predicates before parallel branches are accepted together.
book_failure_modes: Tool choice exceeds authority or budget.
Dispatch laundering, where a proposed or blocked node becomes a job because it appears in the graph.
Replanning erasure, where feedback changes the plan while hiding the authority, stop-condition, or residual delta.
Wrong capability-tier selection, where a cheap route produces plausible but inadequate work.
Dependency cycles, where a graph looks planned while no valid dispatch order exists.
Merge laundering, where parallel branches pass locally while relying on conflicting assumptions or source versions.
Arbitrage laundering, where verification, repair, or human cleanup is moved outside the cost-quality ledger.

book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a compiler toolchain for cognitive work. It turns goals, command contracts, plans, and source context into semantic intermediate representations before producing code, prose, schemas, proof targets, experiments, or tool jobs. The central product is not the generated artifact; it is the obligation-preserving build graph that explains why the artifact should exist and what it must continue to satisfy. In a cognitive compiler, every important artifact has a lowering receipt. The receipt names the source plan, semantic atoms, obligations, assumptions, validators, target constraints, authority requirements, repair scope, support-state effect, and residuals that produced it. A later writer, model, compiler pass, or reviewer can change one node without erasing unrelated obligations or pretending that a syntactic pass is semantic acceptance.

book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_minimal_implementation: A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states.
book_chapter_implementation_crosswalk.book_beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a verification-capacity control plane. It does not reward a system for loading more text.
